skill.name: azure-deploy
skill.description: Deploy prepared and validated applications to Azure using Azure Developer CLI, Azure CLI, Bicep, Terraform, and CI/CD workflows.
skill.summary: Run final Azure deployment workflows after preparation and validation checks are complete.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, credential, filesystem, and network patterns, but most are Markdown deployment examples rather than hidden executable code. The confirmed risk is legitimate but high-impact Azure deployment guidance, including no-prompt provisioning, destructive cleanup examples, CI secrets references, and one curl-to-shell installer pattern. No evidence found of credential exfiltration, obfuscation, or prompt injection that would justify blocking publication.

medium_findings.title: High-Impact Azure Deployment Commands
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill explicitly guides agents to run Azure deployment and provisioning commands. This is expected for the skill, but it can create, modify, or redeploy cloud resources and may incur cost if used without user confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files directly instruct deployment commands such as azd up, azd deploy, az deployment, and terraform apply. The commands match the skill purpose, so the risk is operational impact rather than malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Pipe to Shell Installer Pattern
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The reference guidance installs azd by piping a remote script directly to bash. This creates supply-chain risk because the downloaded script executes immediately without inspection or checksum verification.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell pattern is explicit on the cited line. It is a common installer shortcut, but it remains a real security risk in automated agent guidance.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Cleanup Commands Require Confirmation
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE WITH MITIGATION: The references include destructive cleanup examples such as deleting a resource group. The skill also includes rules requiring user confirmation before destructive actions, which reduces but does not remove the risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The destructive command examples and data-loss warning are explicit. Confidence is slightly lower because the skill provides clear confirmation rules that mitigate accidental execution.

low_findings.title: Credential References Are CI Configuration Guidance
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE FOR EXFILTRATION: The detected Azure credential variables are used in GitHub Actions and Azure identity documentation. No evidence found that the skill sends secrets to an unauthorized endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The references use standard Azure authentication names and GitHub secrets syntax. The files do not contain a network exfiltration path, but handling cloud credentials still requires care.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal and Weak Crypto Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The path traversal alerts correspond to relative Markdown links such as checklist references, not file reads or writes. The weak cryptography alerts appear to be keyword matches in prose or Markdown context, with no cryptographic implementation found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited path references are Markdown navigation, and the reviewed files are documentation rather than source code. No evidence found of filesystem traversal logic or weak cryptographic code.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Review Found No Malicious Override
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE CHECK: The skill uses strong mandatory wording for deployment prerequisites and validation, but no evidence found of instructions to ignore higher-priority rules, skip security review, or claim pre-approval.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed wording is directive but tied to workflow safety and validation. It does not contain the prompt-injection patterns specified for critical findings.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Script Piped to Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The installation reference executes a downloaded script through bash. Prefer package manager installation or checksum verification before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command structure directly matches a pipe-to-shell pattern. This pattern is risky even when the domain is a legitimate Microsoft short link.

dangerous_patterns.title: No-Prompt and Auto-Approve Deployment Modes
dangerous_patterns.description: Several examples use noninteractive deployment flags. These are useful for CI but can bypass interactive safety checks when used manually.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The noninteractive flags are explicit in the examples. The surrounding checklist mitigates risk, but marketplace users should still see a warning.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in Deployment Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Terraform output values are substituted into shell commands. This is normal deployment practice, but unexpected output values could affect command behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The substitution pattern is present, but it consumes local Terraform outputs rather than direct user input. Risk is plausible but context-dependent.

content.value_statement: Azure deployments fail when environment, subscription, and region choices are not verified first. This skill guides final deployment steps with Azure-specific checks, recipes, and recovery guidance.
actual_capabilities: Checks that Azure preparation and validation artifacts exist before deployment.
Guides Azure Developer CLI deployment flows such as azd up, provision, and deploy.
Provides deployment recipes for Azure CLI, Bicep, Terraform, Azure Functions, and CI/CD.
Requires subscription, resource group, and location confirmation before provisioning.
Includes troubleshooting and verification references for deployed Azure endpoints.
limitations: It does not create or design the application before deployment.
It assumes validation has already completed through a separate Azure validation workflow.
It can trigger real Azure resource changes when commands are executed.
It depends on local Azure tools, credentials, and permissions being configured.

use_cases.title: Deploy a validated web app
use_cases.description: Run the final Azure deployment after plan, infrastructure, and environment checks are complete.

use_cases.title: Standardize release steps
use_cases.description: Use repeatable Azure CLI, azd, Bicep, or Terraform recipes for team deployment workflows.

use_cases.title: Prepare pipeline deployment
use_cases.description: Review GitHub Actions and Azure DevOps deployment patterns using Azure credentials and protected environments.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy with azd
prompt_templates.prompt: Deploy this validated project to Azure with azd. Confirm the subscription and region before running deployment commands.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy Bicep infrastructure
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Bicep deployment recipe for this project. Show the planned subscription, location, and deployment command before execution.

prompt_templates.title: Release through CI/CD
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Azure deployment pipeline and identify the required secrets, variables, environment protections, and verification steps.

prompt_templates.title: Recover a failed deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this Azure deployment failure, map it to the relevant recipe, and propose the safest recovery steps before retrying.

output_examples.input: Deploy my validated Azure Functions app.
output_examples.output: The assistant confirms the validated plan, checks the target subscription and region, selects the Azure Functions recipe, then presents deployment and verification steps.

output_examples.input: My Terraform Azure deployment failed after apply.
output_examples.output: The assistant reviews Terraform outputs, resource group state, endpoint health, and common errors before recommending a controlled retry path.

output_examples.input: Prepare a GitHub Actions deployment for Azure.
output_examples.output: The assistant lists required Azure secrets, environment variables, protected environment settings, login steps, deployment actions, and post-deploy checks.

best_practices: Confirm subscription, resource group, and region before every deployment.
Use preview or plan commands before provisioning production resources.
Require explicit user approval for destructive or high-cost actions.
anti_patterns: Running deployment commands before validation proof is available.
Using no-prompt or auto-approve flags without a reviewed plan.
Embedding Azure credentials directly in code, templates, or logs.

faq.question: Does this skill create Azure applications from scratch?
faq.answer: No. It is for final deployment after application preparation and validation are complete.

faq.question: Can this skill deploy with Azure Developer CLI?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes azd workflows for provisioning, full deployment, and application-only deployment.

faq.question: Does it support Bicep and Terraform?
faq.answer: Yes. The references include Azure CLI, Bicep, Terraform, Azure Functions, and CI/CD deployment recipes.

faq.question: Will it ask before destructive operations?
faq.answer: The skill guidance requires explicit user confirmation before delete, overwrite, purge, or costly actions.

faq.question: What credentials are required?
faq.answer: Users need valid Azure authentication through local tools, managed identity, service principals, or federated CI credentials.

faq.question: Why is this marked medium risk?
faq.answer: It can run real cloud deployment commands and includes one pipe-to-shell installer reference, but no malicious behavior was found.
